figures.caption: Table 14: Regularizer ablation on three environments ordered by signal strength. SR (%); “#nz” is the mean number of non-zero fitted weights across three seeds. For this ablation, ϕcand is expanded to a stress-test pool of 31 features (5 universal +3 derived +20 hidden-state PCA +3 auto-extracted) to evaluate each regularizer’s behavior under a high-dimensional candidate set; adding the LLM feature layer brings the pool to ≈36. ℓ1 is the only variant that achieves competitive SR and exact sparsity required by the signed-weight diagnostic.
Table 8: Detailed comparison of baselines. All methods assume a fixed signal-utility direction. †Requires a separate labeled dataset for calibration.

Table 9: Computational cost breakdown per method. Gate OH = additional LLM calls per step beyond the base proposer. Cost (×base) = total deployment tokens normalized by base only tokens per episode, averaged across 6 environments. Calibration/exploration overhead is excluded for all methods. †Requires separate calibration data.

Figure 8: Multi-backbone signal direction heatmap. ∗ marks cells significant at p<0.05. Cells marked with ⇄ are sign-flip cells: the same (environment, signal) pair yields a significantly positive ρ on at least one backbone and a significantly negative ρ on another, i.e. the signal–utility direction is reversed across model families. Entropy direction is model-dependent; structural signals (step count) are more stable.
Figure 9: Cross-backbone comparison: DIAL (red) vs. best fixed-direction baseline (blue) across 3 backbones. Fixed baselines show large SR variance across backbones; DIAL adapts robustly.

Table 18: Temporal shift in ρ(entropy, U ). All environments show ρ shifting more negative from early to late steps, consistent with P1. Plancraft omitted (|ρ∗|=0.016, too weak for meaningful comparison).
Figure 7: SR vs. Cost (×base) across all 6 environments on Qwen3-4B. Shaded region: dominated by DIAL (lower SR and higher cost). Full numerical results across three backbones are in Table 2.
Table 11: Datasets and pre-trained models used in this paper.

Table 17: Robustness of ρ(σ, U) to monotone transforms. Spearman ρ is rank-invariant under monotone transforms of either variable, so the σ0.5, σ2, and log σ columns must match raw up to numerical noise (a sanity check on the entropy pipeline); under U → αU with α < 0, sign flips as expected. Pearson ρ varies under non-linear σ transforms; the only Pearson sign flip is Phi-3.5/APPS under log σ (+0.027), where Spearman stays stable at −0.129. Excluded: three cells with |σunique|≤2 or no positive U labels (TWExpress on {Phi-3.5, Llama-3.1}, Plancraft on Phi-3.5), where correlation is ill-defined.
Figure 5: Prompt template used in the LLM feature layer (Phase 2) to propose task-specific features from exploration data.

Table 16: Robustness of cross-backbone reversal to entropy quantile-normalization. We report Spearman ρ(σ, U) (invariant under within-cell monotone transforms, so shared across raw/S1/S2/S3) and Pearson ρ under raw and three quantile schemes: S1 ranks within each (env, backbone), S2 within each backbone’s pool, S3 within each environment’s pool. Listed are the 11 strong-signal cells (raw Pearson |ρ| ≥ 0.13) covering every reversal claim. Robust = sign stable vs. raw under all three schemes (CIs on the same side). ‡ Phi-3.5 APPS robust under S1, S2 but inconclusive under S3. Excluded (discussed in prose): 4 weak-signal cells (|ρ| < 0.13, raw CIs straddle zero) and 3 cells with insufficient logged data. ∗: p < 0.05, 1000-resample bootstrap.
Figure 6: Complete LLM-generated feature extraction function for WebShop (seed 42), the environment with the most LLM features retained by LASSO.

Figure 10: Environment-adaptive trigger behavior. Panels ordered by rollout headroom ∆. Solid line: per-step trigger rate with 95% CI band. Dashed gray: 50% reference. RR: overall trigger rate. DIAL’s trigger rate ranges from 73% (TWExpress) to <20% (late-step Plancraft), emerging from direction learning alone.

Figure 2: The DIAL pipeline. (a) Explore: paired counterfactual rollouts via Bernoulli-ε yield raw data Draw={(state text, R(aT )−R(aB))}. (b) Reason: from each state text, a candidate feature pool ϕcand(s) combines universal features with LLM-proposed task-specific features, yielding D={(ϕ(s), U)}. (c) Learn: fit an ℓ1-regularized logistic gate g(s); signed weights jointly select features and recover per-environment direction (Type D: wi>0; Type I: wi<0).
